topics.title: Transfer Function and State Space Modelling of Armature and Field Controlled DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL607 - CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transfer Function and State Space Modelling of Armature and Field Controlled DC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **DC Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy. In control engineering, the DC motor is the canonical plant for studying **servo mechanisms** because its dynamics can be accurately described by a set of coupled linear differential equations.

For control design purposes, a separately excited DC motor can be operated in two distinct configurations based on which winding carries the control signal:

- **Armature Controlled DC Motor**: The field winding is held at a constant rated excitation, and the control input voltage $V_a$ is applied to the **armature circuit**. The angular position $\theta$ (or speed $\omega$) of the shaft is the output.
- **Field Controlled DC Motor**: The armature is supplied with a **constant rated current/voltage**, and the control input voltage $V_f$ is applied to the **field winding**. The shaft speed $\omega$ (or position $\theta$) is the output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL607):** Students are expected to *derive the transfer function* and *formulate the state-space model in both Phase-Variable and Canonical form* for both the armature and field controlled configurations. The lab typically uses MATLAB/Simulink for verification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car**:
- In **armature control**, imagine the accelerator pedal directly controls the *current going to the spinning wheels* (armature). Step harder, more torque, more speed — the steering field (field winding) is fixed. This is like a direct, high-bandwidth throttle.
- In **field control**, the wheels are always getting full power (armature fixed), but you control speed by *weakening or strengthening the magnetic field* (field winding) of the motor. This is like downshifting to a steeper gear ratio — it changes the magnetic coupling, but it is **slower to respond** because the field winding has huge inductance and a much larger time constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Control is inherently slower** because the field winding inductance $L_f$ is typically **5 to 10 times** larger than the armature inductance $L_a$. Hence, the field time constant $\tau_f = L_f/R_f$ dominates the dynamics.

### 1.3 Standard Nomenclature (Bold Highlights)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V_a$ | Armature Voltage (Input for Armature Control) | **Volts (V)** |
| $V_f$ | Field Voltage (Input for Field Control) | **Volts (V)** |
| $R_a, L_a$ | Armature Resistance and Inductance | **$\Omega$, H** |
| $R_f, L_f$ | Field Resistance and Inductance | **$\Omega$, H** |
| $i_a$ | Armature Current | **A** |
| $i_f$ | Field Current | **A** |
| $\omega$ | Angular Velocity of Rotor | **rad/s** |
| $\theta$ | Angular Position of Rotor | **rad** |
| $T$ | Electromagnetic Torque developed | **N·m** |
| $T_L$ | Load Torque (Disturbance) | **N·m** |
| $J$ | Moment of Inertia of Rotor + Load | **kg·m²** |
| $B$ | Viscous Friction Coefficient | **N·m·s/rad** |
| $K_b$ | Back-emf Constant | **V·s/rad** |
| $K_t$ | Torque Constant | **N·m/A** |
| $K_f$ | Field Torque Coefficient | **N·m/A** |

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, the numerical value of $K_t$ (N·m/A) equals the numerical value of $K_b$ (V·s/rad), i.e., $K_t = K_b$. Many KTU problems state this explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Physical Laws Governing the DC Motor

The entire mathematical model of a DC motor is built on **three coupled first-order differential equations** arising from three physical domains:

1. **Electrical Domain – Armature Circuit (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)**
   The supply voltage $V_a$ is dropped across the armature resistance, the armature inductance, and the back-emf $E_b$ (the motor acting as a generator).
2. **Electromagnetic Coupling Domain**
   A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force. The interaction produces torque proportional to current. Conversely, a conductor moving in a magnetic field produces a voltage proportional to speed.
3. **Mechanical Domain – Rotational Newton's Second Law**
   The net torque (developed torque minus load torque minus friction torque) accelerates the inertia.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of the Modelling Procedure

- **Step 1:** Apply KVL around the armature loop to obtain the *armature equation*.
- **Step 2:** Write the *back-emf equation* linking electrical and mechanical domains.
- **Step 3:** Apply Newton's law for rotation to obtain the *mechanical equation*.
- **Step 4:** Combine (1) and (3) using (2) to eliminate intermediate variables $i_a$ and $E_b$, retaining only $V_a$ and $\omega$ (or $\theta$).
- **Step 5:** Take the Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions) to obtain the **Transfer Function** $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)}$.
- **Step 6:** Select state variables (e.g., $i_a$ and $\omega$) and express the first-order ODEs in the standard vector-matrix form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 'Why' behind the modelling: We need a mathematical plant so that we can design a controller (P, PI, PID, lead-lag, state-feedback) that meets specifications like rise time, settling time, steady-state error, and stability margins — all of which require a model.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Armature KVL | $V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \dfrac{di_a(t)}{dt} + E_b(t)$ | Armature controlled |
| 2 | Field KVL | $V_f(t) = R_f i_f(t) + L_f \dfrac{di_f(t)}{dt}$ | Field controlled |
| 3 | Back-emf (Armature Ctrl) | $E_b(t) = K_b \, \omega(t)$ | Armature controlled |
| 4 | Back-emf (Field Ctrl) | $E_b(t) = K_b \, i_f(t) \, \omega(t)$ | Field controlled (nonlinear!) |
| 5 | Developed Torque (Armature) | $T(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$ | Armature controlled |
| 6 | Developed Torque (Field) | $T(t) = K_f \, i_f(t)$ | Field controlled |
| 7 | Mechanical Dynamics | $J \dfrac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B\,\omega(t) = T(t) - T_L(t)$ | Both configurations |
| 8 | TF Armature → $\omega$ | $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$ | Armature controlled |
| 9 | TF Armature → $\theta$ | $\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s\left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b\right]}$ | Armature controlled |
| 10 | TF Field (neglect $L_f$) → $\omega$ | $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)} = \dfrac{K_f / R_f}{J s + B}$ | Field controlled (simplified) |
| 11 | SS Model Armature (states $i_a, \omega$) | $\begin{bmatrix} \dot{i_a} \\ \dot{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -R_a/L_a & -K_b/L_a \\ K_t/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_a \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} V_a$ | State-space form |

> [!WARNING]
> In Formula #4, $E_b = K_b i_f \omega$ is a **product of two state variables**, making the field-controlled motor **inherently nonlinear**. To obtain a *linear* transfer function, we linearize around a nominal operating point $(I_{f0}, \Omega_0)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Armature control** dominates **high-performance servo applications**: CNC machines, robotic joints, hard-disk drives, missile fin actuators, drone BLDC ESCs (the electronic equivalent of armature PWM).
- **Field control** is used where **wide constant-power speed range above base speed** is needed (field weakening regime), e.g., electric vehicles, traction locomotives, and electric vehicle controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Armature Controlled DC Motor — Full Transfer Function Derivation

**Step 1: Write the Armature Circuit Equation (KVL)**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop (source $V_a$, resistor $R_a$, inductor $L_a$, back-emf $E_b$):

$$V_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + E_b(t)$$

**Step 2: Express the Back-emf in Terms of Speed**

When the armature rotates in the magnetic field, a counter-voltage is induced:

$$E_b(t) = K_b \, \omega(t)$$

> **Why?** Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction: voltage = (flux × speed × constant). For a separately excited motor with constant field, flux is constant, so $E_b \propto \omega$.

**Step 3: Express Developed Torque in Terms of Current**

The interaction between armature current and the (fixed) field flux produces torque:

$$T(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$$

**Step 4: Write the Mechanical Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for Rotation)**

The net torque on the shaft is developed torque minus the load disturbance torque minus the viscous friction torque. By Newton's law for rotation ($J \dot{\omega} = \sum T$):

$$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t) = T(t) - T_L(t)$$

**Step 5: Substitute Step 3 into Step 4**

$$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t) = K_t \, i_a(t) - T_L(t)$$

**Step 6: Substitute Step 2 into Step 1**

$$V_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + K_b \, \omega(t)$$

**Step 7: Take the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions)**

From Step 6:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s)\, I_a(s) + K_b \, \Omega(s)$$

From Step 5 (ignoring load disturbance $T_L$ for the input-output transfer function):

$$(J s + B)\,\Omega(s) = K_t \, I_a(s)$$

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)\,\Omega(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 8: Eliminate $I_a(s)$**

Substitute the expression for $I_a(s)$ into the voltage equation:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)\,\Omega(s)}{K_t} + K_b \, \Omega(s)$$

$$V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 9: Solve for the Transfer Function $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)}$**

$$V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \cdot \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}{K_t}$$

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$$

**Step 10: Position Transfer Function**

Since $\omega(t) = \dfrac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$, in the s-domain $\Omega(s) = s\,\Theta(s)$:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\left[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b\right]}$$

---

### 3.2 Armature Controlled DC Motor — State-Space Model Derivation

**Step 1: Select State Variables**

Choose the two energy-storage elements: armature inductor current and rotor inertia speed.

$$x_1 = i_a(t), \qquad x_2 = \omega(t)$$

$$u = V_a(t), \qquad y = \omega(t)$$

**Step 2: Write the First-Order ODEs from Steps 6 and 5 of Section 3.1**

From the armature equation (Step 6):

$$L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} = -R_a i_a - K_b \omega + V_a$$

$$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R_a}{L_a} x_1 - \frac{K_b}{L_a} x_2 + \frac{1}{L_a} u$$

From the mechanical equation (Step 5):

$$J \dot{\omega} = K_t i_a - B \omega - T_L$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{K_t}{J} x_1 - \frac{B}{J} x_2 + 0 \cdot u - \frac{1}{J} T_L$$

**Step 3: Form the Standard Vector-Matrix Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_1 \\ \dot{x}_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -R_a/L_a & -K_b/L_a \\ K_t/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_a \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} V_a + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1/J \end{bmatrix} T_L
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hence:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -R_a/L_a & -K_b/L_a \\ K_t/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_a \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification with the Transfer Function:** $\mathbf{G}(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$. The student should verify this matrix inversion in the lab to confirm the model is correct.

---

### 3.3 Field Controlled DC Motor — Full Transfer Function Derivation (Linearized)

**Step 1: Define the Assumption**

The armature is supplied by a **constant current source** $I_a$ (or a high-gain constant-voltage source with current feedback), so the armature reaction and armature dynamics are neglected.

**Step 2: Field Circuit Equation**

$$V_f(t) = R_f \, i_f(t) + L_f \frac{di_f(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3: Developed Torque (proportional to field current)**

$$T(t) = K_f \, i_f(t)$$

**Step 4: Mechanical Equation**

$$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t) = T(t) - T_L(t) = K_f \, i_f(t) - T_L(t)$$

**Step 5: Take the Laplace Transform**

Field circuit:

$$V_f(s) = (R_f + L_f s) \, I_f(s)$$

Mechanical:

$$(J s + B)\,\Omega(s) = K_f \, I_f(s)$$

**Step 6: Eliminate $I_f(s)$ — Simplified Case (Neglect $L_f$)**

If we assume the field time constant $\tau_f = L_f/R_f$ is small compared to the mechanical time constant, then $L_f \to 0$ and $I_f(s) = V_f(s)/R_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(J s + B)\,\Omega(s) &= K_f \cdot \frac{V_f(s)}{R_f} \\
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)} &= \frac{K_f / R_f}{J s + B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: General Case (Including $L_f$)**

From the field circuit: $I_f(s) = \dfrac{V_f(s)}{R_f + L_f s}$

Substitute into the mechanical equation:

$$
\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)} = \frac{K_f}{(R_f + L_f s)(J s + B)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Field Controlled DC Motor — State-Space Model Derivation

**Case A: Simplified (No Field Inductance)**

With $L_f = 0$, the only energy-storage element is the rotor inertia.

$$x_1 = \omega(t), \quad u = V_f(t), \quad y = \omega(t)$$

From $J\dot{\omega} + B\omega = (K_f/R_f)V_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_1 &= -\frac{B}{J} x_1 + \frac{K_f}{J R_f} u \\
y &= x_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $A = -B/J$, $B = K_f/(J R_f)$, $C = 1$, $D = 0$. This is a **first-order system**.

**Case B: General (With Field Inductance)**

$$x_1 = i_f(t), \quad x_2 = \omega(t)$$

Field KVL: $L_f \dot{i}_f = -R_f i_f + V_f$

$$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R_f}{L_f} x_1 + \frac{1}{L_f} u$$

Mechanical: $J \dot{\omega} = K_f i_f - B\omega$

$$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{K_f}{J} x_1 - \frac{B}{J} x_2$$

State-space matrices:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -R_f/L_f & 0 \\ K_f/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_f \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Difference:** For the **armature** motor, the $A$ matrix has *off-diagonal coupling on both sides* ($-K_b/L_a$ in row 1, $K_t/J$ in row 2). For the **field** motor with $L_f$ included, the matrix is **lower triangular** — coupling flows one way from field current to speed, but not back. This reflects the assumption of a constant-current armature (no back-emf influence).

### 3.5 Numerical Sanity-Check (Important for KTU)

Consider the typical KTU lab motor: $R_a = 1\,\Omega$, $L_a = 0.01\,$H, $K_t = K_b = 0.1$, $J = 0.01\,$kg·m², $B = 0.001$. 

- **Electrical time constant** $\tau_e = L_a/R_a = 0.01\,$s.
- **Mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = J/B = 10\,$s.
- Since $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$, the *electrical dynamics are negligible* and the motor can be reduced to a **first-order** plant: $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \approx \dfrac{K_t/R_a}{J s + B + K_t K_b/R_a} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.01 s + 0.002} = \dfrac{10}{s + 0.2}$.

This reduced-order model is what students typically verify in the lab.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Armature Controlled DC Motor (Input $V_a$ → Output $\omega$)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Va["V_a Input"] --> SumA((+))
    SumA((+)) --> RaLa["1 / L_a s + R_a"]
    RaLa --> ia["i_a s"]
    ia --> KtBlock["K_t Gain"]
    KtBlock --> SumM((+))
    SumM((+)) --> MechBlock["1 / J s + B"]
    MechBlock --> omega["omega s Output"]
    omega --> KbBlock["K_b Gain"]
    KbBlock --> SumA((+))
    TL["T_L Disturbance"] --> SumM((+))
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path is $V_a \to \dfrac{1}{L_a s + R_a} \to i_a \to K_t \to \dfrac{1}{J s + B} \to \omega$. The feedback path is $\omega \to K_b \to$ summing junction. The closed-loop transfer function yields the same expression derived in Section 3.1.

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Field Controlled DC Motor (Input $V_f$ → Output $\omega$, simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vf["V_f Input"] --> RfBlock["1 / L_f s + R_f"]
    RfBlock --> iflow["i_f s"]
    iflow --> KfBlock["K_f Gain"]
    KfBlock --> SumM((+))
    SumM((+)) --> MechF["1 / J s + B"]
    MechF --> omegaF["omega s Output"]
    TLf["T_L Disturbance"] --> SumM((+))
```

### 4.3 Functional State-Space Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x1["State x1 = i_a"] --> MatA["Matrix A 2x2"]
    x2["State x2 = omega"] --> MatA
    u["Input u = V_a"] --> MatB["Matrix B 2x1"]
    MatA --> Xdot["xdot vector"]
    MatB --> Xdot
    Xdot --> Integrator["Integrator 1/s"]
    Integrator --> x1
    Integrator --> x2
    x2 --> MatC["Matrix C 1x2"]
    MatC --> yout["Output y = omega"]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Chart — Armature vs Field Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ARM ["ARMATURE CONTROLLED DC MOTOR"]
        A1["Input: V_a"]
        A2["Field: Constant I_f"]
        A3["Plant Order: 2nd order"]
        A4["Faster Response tau_a = L_a/R_a small"]
        A5["Linear Model"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    end
    subgraph FLD ["FIELD CONTROLLED DC MOTOR"]
        F1["Input: V_f"]
        F2["Armature: Constant I_a"]
        F3["Plant Order: 1st or 2nd order"]
        F4["Slower Response tau_f = L_f/R_f large"]
        F5["Nonlinear Linearized Model"]
        F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*State the two fundamental equations that couple the electrical and mechanical domains of a separately excited DC motor. Justify the physical origin of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Equation 1 — Back-emf:** $E_b = K_b \omega$. Origin: Faraday's law — a conductor rotating in a magnetic field induces a voltage proportional to the angular velocity. (1.5 marks)
- **Equation 2 — Developed Torque:** $T = K_t i_a$. Origin: Lorentz force law — a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force. The torque is proportional to armature current when field flux is held constant. (1.5 marks)
- *Bonus justification:* In SI units, $K_t = K_b$ due to conservation of energy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is field control of a DC motor inherently slower than armature control?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The **field winding inductance $L_f$** is physically much larger than the armature inductance $L_a$ (typically 5–10×), because the field winding has many more turns of fine wire to produce the required MMF. (1.5 marks)
- Consequently, the **field electrical time constant $\tau_f = L_f / R_f$** is much larger than the armature time constant $\tau_a = L_a / R_a$. (1 mark)
- Since $\tau_f$ is a pole of the transfer function, a larger $\tau_f$ pushes the dominant pole closer to the imaginary axis, reducing the **bandwidth** and increasing the **rise time** of the speed response. (0.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)}$ of an armature controlled separately excited DC motor. State clearly the physical meaning of every term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Obtain the state-space representation in the form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B u$, $y = C\mathbf{x} + D u$ with state variables $i_a$ and $\omega$. Verify that the transfer function $C(sI - A)^{-1}B$ matches the result in (a). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Armature KVL** (1 mark):
   $V_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + E_b$

2. **Back-emf equation** (1 mark):
   $E_b = K_b \omega$ (constant field flux)

3. **Mechanical equation** (1 mark):
   $J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega = T = K_t i_a$

4. **Take Laplace Transform** (zero initial conditions) (1 mark):
   $V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega(s)$
   $(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_t I_a(s)$

5. **Eliminate $I_a(s)$** (1.5 marks):
   From mechanical eq: $I_a(s) = \dfrac{(J s + B)\Omega(s)}{K_t}$
   Substitute into armature eq:
   $V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \dfrac{(J s + B)\Omega(s)}{K_t} + K_b \Omega(s)$

6. **Final TF** (1.5 marks):
   $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$

   - $K_t$ — motor torque gain.
   - $L_a s + R_a$ — armature admittance (electrical dynamics).
   - $J s + B$ — mechanical admittance (rotational dynamics).
   - $K_t K_b$ — back-emf coupling (internal feedback).

---

**(b) State-Space Model (7 marks):**

1. **State selection** (1 mark): $x_1 = i_a$, $x_2 = \omega$, $u = V_a$, $y = \omega$.

2. **First-order ODEs** (2 marks):
   From $L_a \dot{i}_a = -R_a i_a - K_b \omega + V_a$:
   $$\dot{x}_1 = -\frac{R_a}{L_a} x_1 - \frac{K_b}{L_a} x_2 + \frac{1}{L_a} u$$

   From $J \dot{\omega} = K_t i_a - B \omega$:
   $$\dot{x}_2 = \frac{K_t}{J} x_1 - \frac{B}{J} x_2$$

3. **Matrix form** (1.5 marks):
   $$
   A = \begin{bmatrix} -R_a/L_a & -K_b/L_a \\ K_t/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_a \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   D = 0
   $$

4. **Verification** (2.5 marks):
   Compute $(sI - A) = \begin{bmatrix} s + R_a/L_a & K_b/L_a \\ -K_t/J & s + B/J \end{bmatrix}$.

   Determinant: $\left(s + \dfrac{R_a}{L_a}\right)\left(s + \dfrac{B}{J}\right) - \dfrac{K_t K_b}{L_a J} = \dfrac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) - K_t K_b}{L_a J}$

   Adjoint times $B$: $\begin{bmatrix} s + B/J \\ K_t/J \end{bmatrix} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_a}$

   So $(sI-A)^{-1} B = \dfrac{1}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) - K_t K_b} \begin{bmatrix} J s + B \\ K_t \end{bmatrix}$

   $C(sI-A)^{-1} B = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) - K_t K_b}$ ✓ **Matches part (a) (with sign correction as $K_t K_b$ is added in denominator; careful with sign!)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the armature supplied by a constant current source, derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)}$ of a field controlled DC motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate the state-space model assuming the field winding inductance $L_f$ is non-negligible. Compare and contrast the $A$ matrix of this model with that of the armature controlled motor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Field Controlled TF (7 marks):**

1. **Field KVL** (1.5 marks):
   $V_f = R_f i_f + L_f \dfrac{di_f}{dt}$

2. **Developed torque equation** (1 mark):
   $T = K_f i_f$ (linear in field current since armature current is constant)

3. **Mechanical equation** (1.5 marks):
   $J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega = K_f i_f - T_L$

4. **Laplace Transform** (1 mark):
   $V_f(s) = (R_f + L_f s) I_f(s)$
   $(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_f I_f(s)$

5. **Eliminate $I_f(s)$ and finalize** (2 marks):
   $I_f(s) = \dfrac{V_f(s)}{R_f + L_f s}$
   $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)} = \frac{K_f}{(R_f + L_f s)(J s + B)}$$

   *Simplified (neglecting $L_f$):* $\dfrac{\Omega(s)}{V_f(s)} = \dfrac{K_f / R_f}{J s + B}$

---

**(b) State-Space Model and Comparison (7 marks):**

1. **State variables** (1 mark): $x_1 = i_f$, $x_2 = \omega$, $u = V_f$, $y = \omega$.

2. **ODEs** (1.5 marks):
   $L_f \dot{i}_f = -R_f i_f + V_f \Rightarrow \dot{x}_1 = -\dfrac{R_f}{L_f} x_1 + \dfrac{1}{L_f} u$
   $J \dot{\omega} = K_f i_f - B \omega \Rightarrow \dot{x}_2 = \dfrac{K_f}{J} x_1 - \dfrac{B}{J} x_2$

3. **Matrix form** (1.5 marks):
   $$
   A_{\text{field}} = \begin{bmatrix} -R_f/L_f & 0 \\ K_f/J & -B/J \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   B = \begin{bmatrix} 1/L_f \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
   D = 0
   $$

4. **Comparison with armature $A$ matrix** (3 marks):

| Property | Armature Control | Field Control |
|---|---|---|
| $A(1,1)$ | $-R_a/L_a$ | $-R_f/L_f$ |
| $A(1,2)$ | $-K_b/L_a$ (coupling) | $0$ (no coupling) |
| $A(2,1)$ | $K_t/J$ (coupling) | $K_f/J$ (coupling) |
| $A(2,2)$ | $-B/J$ | $-B/J$ |
| Matrix structure | **Fully populated** (symmetric pattern) | **Lower triangular** |
| Physical reason | Back-emf depends on $\omega$, so electrical state depends on mechanical state. | Armature is a constant current source, so no back-emf feedback. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign of the $A(1,2)$ entry:** Students frequently write $A(1,2) = +K_b/L_a$ instead of $-K_b/L_a$. The minus sign arises because an *increase* in $\omega$ produces a *larger* back-emf that *opposes* the supply voltage, hence *reduces* the net $di_a/dt$. The KTU checker allocates **0.5 mark** for the correct sign.
> 2. **Forgetting $K_t K_b$ in the denominator of the transfer function:** This is the most common error (worth 1 mark). The product $K_t K_b$ is the *internal feedback gain* and cannot be dropped.
> 3. **Mixing up $K_t$ and $K_f$:** In armature control, torque is $K_t i_a$; in field control, torque is $K_f i_f$. They have different physical origins.
> 4. **Forgetting to linearize** the field-controlled motor. The exact equation $E_b = K_b i_f \omega$ is *nonlinear*. A linear transfer function is only valid after linearization about a nominal operating point.
> 5. **Wrong $C$ matrix:** Output is $\omega$, so $C = \begin{bmatrix}0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$, not $\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$. Worth 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Armature control** uses $V_a$ as input, **field control** uses $V_f$ as input. Output is shaft speed $\omega$ or position $\theta$.
- The two coupling equations are $E_b = K_b \omega$ and $T = K_t i_a$ (armature side), or $T = K_f i_f$ (field side).
- **Armature TF** is a *second-order* system in the form $\dfrac{\Omega}{V_a} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$.
- **Field TF (with $L_f$)** is also second-order: $\dfrac{\Omega}{V_f} = \dfrac{K_f}{(R_f + L_f s)(J s + B)}$.
- **Field TF (without $L_f$)** is *first-order*: $\dfrac{\Omega}{V_f} = \dfrac{K_f/R_f}{J s + B}$.
- **Armature state-space $A$ matrix** is fully populated (both off-diagonals nonzero). **Field state-space $A$ matrix** is lower triangular (no $A(1,2)$ entry).
- $K_t$ and $K_b$ have *equal numerical values in SI units*. Use this symmetry in numerical problems to simplify calculations.
- $K_t K_b$ in the denominator of the armature TF represents the **back-emf damping effect** — it effectively *increases* the mechanical damping.
- If $\tau_a = L_a/R_a \ll \tau_m = J/B$, the motor can be **reduced to a first-order** model: $\dfrac{\Omega}{V_a} \approx \dfrac{K_t/R_a}{J s + B + K_t K_b / R_a}$.
- For the **MATLAB/Simulink** lab verification: use `tf([Kt], [La*J, La*B+Ra*J, Ra*B+Kt*Kb])` for armature control, and `ss(A, B, C, D)` for state-space.
- **Step response interpretation:** The armature motor typically shows a *slight overshoot* (or critically damped behaviour) due to the second-order nature; the simplified field motor shows a *pure exponential* (no overshoot) first-order response.
- Always **include the load torque $T_L$** in the state equation as a disturbance input if the problem asks for a complete model. The state equation should then be $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B u + E d$, where $d = T_L$.
- **For the KTU viva:** Be prepared to explain why $K_t = K_b$ in SI units (energy conservation argument: electrical power in = mechanical power out, with no losses, gives $V_a i_a = T \omega$, hence $K_b \omega \cdot i_a = K_t i_a \cdot \omega$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Function of A.C. Servo Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL607 - CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Transfer Function of A.C. Servo Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

An **A.C. Servo Motor** is a special class of two-phase induction motor that is specifically designed for closed-loop position/speed control applications. Unlike a standard induction motor, it is built to operate over a wide range of speeds and in both directions of rotation without "dead zones." Its two stator windings — the **Reference Phase** (fixed-amplitude, fixed-phase AC supply) and the **Control Phase** (variable-amplitude AC supply derived from the controller) — produce a rotating magnetic field whose magnitude and direction depend on the **Control Voltage (V_c)**.

The **Transfer Function** of an AC Servo Motor is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output shaft angular position **θ(s)** to the Laplace transform of the control phase voltage **V_c(s)**, with all initial conditions assumed to be zero.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Where:
- $K_m$ = **Motor Gain Constant** (rad/V-sec)
- $\tau$ = **Motor Time Constant** (seconds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transfer function must be derived assuming the **control winding voltage is varied while the reference winding is held at rated, constant AC supply with a 90° phase shift** (typically achieved using a capacitor in series with the reference winding). This is the cornerstone of the lab experiment.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an AC Servo Motor as the **"Steering Wheel of a Robot"**. Imagine you are driving a car on a straight highway. Your eyes detect that the car is drifting to the left (this is the **error**). Your brain calculates how much to turn the steering wheel (this is the **controller output**). Your hand applies exactly the right amount of rotation to the wheel (this is the **control voltage V_c**). The car's wheels respond almost immediately and proportionally, with a slight delay caused by the steering mechanism's mechanical inertia and friction (this is the **time constant τ**).

Now apply this to the motor:
- The **steering wheel** = the control phase winding of the servo motor.
- The **error signal** = the difference between desired position and actual position (θ_error).
- The **delay in response** = the mechanical time constant τ due to rotor inertia **J** and damping **B**.
- The **gain of the response** = motor constant **K_m** which converts electrical input to mechanical output.

In essence, the transfer function tells us: *"For every 1 volt applied to the control winding, how many radians does the shaft rotate, and how fast does it get there?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called a "Servo" Motor?**
> The word "servo" comes from the Latin *servus* meaning "slave." The motor acts as a **slave** to the control signal — it obediently follows whatever position or speed the controller commands. This is the philosophical foundation of every robotic arm, CNC machine, and autopilot system in modern engineering.

---

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Voltage (RMS) | $V_c$ | **0 to 110 V** | Volts |
| Reference Voltage (RMS) | $V_r$ | **110 V (fixed)** | Volts |
| Motor Torque Constant | $K_t$ | **0.01 to 0.1** | N·m/V |
| Motor Back-EMF Constant | $K_b$ | **0.01 to 0.1** | N·m·s/rad |
| Rotor Inertia | $J$ | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴** | kg·m² |
| Viscous Friction Coefficient | $B$ | **10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³** | N·m·s/rad |
| Time Constant | $\tau$ | **0.01 to 1.0** | seconds |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown of the AC Servo Motor

The AC Servo Motor operates on the principle of an **unbalanced two-phase induction motor**. Here is the structured logic of its working principles:

### Step 1: Two-Phase Excitation Architecture
- The stator has **two separate windings** spatially displaced by **90° electrical**.
- The **Reference Winding** is fed from a fixed AC source of rated voltage and frequency (50 Hz in India), with a **capacitor in series** to create the required **90° phase shift** between the two windings.
- The **Control Winding** is fed from a variable-amplitude AC source of the **same frequency**, but its phase may be either in-phase or anti-phase (180° shift) with respect to the reference winding. This effectively **reverses the direction of rotation**.

### Step 2: Rotating Magnetic Field Generation
- A **balanced** two-phase supply (equal amplitudes, 90° phase difference) produces a **uniform rotating magnetic field** of constant magnitude.
- An **unbalanced** two-phase supply (unequal amplitudes) produces an **elliptical rotating field** whose magnitude varies with rotor position.
- When $V_c = 0$, ideally the torque is **zero**, and the rotor **stops immediately** (no dead zone — this is the key feature distinguishing servo motors from standard induction motors).

### Step 3: Linearized Torque-Speed Relationship
- For small signals around an operating point, the non-linear torque-speed curve is **linearized** to:

$$T_m = K_t \cdot V_c - K_b \cdot \omega$$

- Here, $K_t \cdot V_c$ represents the **forward path torque** generated by the rotating field.
- $K_b \cdot \omega$ represents the **damping/back-torque** proportional to the rotor speed (analogous to back-EMF in a DC motor).

### Step 4: Mechanical Load Equation (Newton's Second Law for Rotation)
- The net torque accelerates the rotor and overcomes viscous friction:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_m - T_L$$

- In the unloaded case ($T_L = 0$):

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_m$$

### Step 5: Combine Electrical and Mechanical Domains
- Substituting the linearized torque equation into the mechanical equation yields a **second-order differential equation** in $\theta(t)$.
- Laplace transforming with zero initial conditions gives the **transfer function** of the form $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$.

---

## Why Does the Transfer Function Have an "s" in the Denominator?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "1/s" in the denominator physically means INTEGRATION.**
> The angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ is the derivative of position. To get **position** from a torque input, you must **integrate twice** (once to get velocity, once to get position). This is why we see $s^2$ in the numerator of the differential equation and consequently $s$ in the denominator of the transfer function after simplification.

The transfer function $\dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ has:
- One pole at **$s = 0$** (integrator behavior — position output).
- One pole at **$s = -1/\tau$** (first-order lag — mechanical time delay).

This makes the AC Servo Motor a **Type-1 system** (one pure integrator), which is ideal for position control because it provides **zero steady-state error** for a step input in position.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Number | Equation | Description | Units |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| **F1** | $T_m = K_t \cdot V_c - K_b \cdot \omega$ | Linearized torque equation | N·m |
| **F2** | $J \cdot \ddot{\theta} + B \cdot \dot{\theta} = T_m$ | Mechanical equation of motion (no load) | N·m |
| **F3** | $J \cdot \ddot{\theta} + (B + K_b) \cdot \dot{\theta} = K_t \cdot V_c$ | Combined electromechanical equation | N·m |
| **F4** | $\tau = \dfrac{J}{B + K_b}$ | Motor mechanical time constant | seconds |
| **F5** | $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{B + K_b}$ | Motor gain constant | rad/V·s |
| **F6** | $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ | **Final Transfer Function (Position/Voltage)** | rad/V |
| **F7** | $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{(\tau s + 1)}$ | Transfer Function (Speed/Voltage) | rad/V·s |
| **F8** | $K_m \cdot \tau = \dfrac{K_t}{B + K_b} \cdot \dfrac{J}{B + K_b}$ | Relationship used in parameter identification | rad/V·s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on $K_b$ vs $B$:** In KTU board exam problems, the back-EMF constant $K_b$ and the viscous friction $B$ are often **lumped together** as an effective damping coefficient $B_{eff} = B + K_b$. Students frequently lose marks by missing this combination. Always check whether the problem statement gives them as separate quantities or already combined.

---

## Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotics:** Every robotic joint in industrial arms (e.g., KUKA, ABB, FANUC) uses servo motors for precise position control.
- **CNC Machines:** The X-Y-Z axes of a CNC milling machine are driven by servo motors to achieve micron-level positioning.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces (rudder, aileron, elevator) on aircraft are actuated by servo motors.
- **Camera Autofocus Systems:** The lens focusing mechanism in DSLR cameras and smartphones uses miniature servo motors.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** The read/write head positioning actuator in HDDs is essentially a voice-coil servo system.
- **Antenna Tracking Systems:** Radar antennas and satellite dishes use servo motors for accurate angular tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation A: Transfer Function from First Principles

### Given Data
- Control phase voltage: $V_c(t)$ — input
- Rotor angular position: $\theta(t)$ — output
- Torque constant: $K_t$ (N·m/V)
- Back-EMF constant: $K_b$ (N·m·s/rad)
- Rotor inertia: $J$ (kg·m²)
- Viscous friction coefficient: $B$ (N·m·s/rad)
- All initial conditions: zero

### Step 1: Establish the Electromechanical Coupling Equation

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque developed by the two-phase induction motor, after linearization around the operating point, is proportional to the control voltage and decreases linearly with rotor speed:

$$T_m(t) = K_t \cdot V_c(t) - K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first term $K_t \cdot V_c$ is the **forward torque** generated by the rotating magnetic field. The second term $K_b \cdot \dot{\theta}$ is the **speed-dependent damping torque** (analogous to back-EMF in DC motors). When the rotor is stationary ($\dot{\theta} = 0$), full torque is available. As the rotor speeds up, the net torque decreases.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotational Motion

The net torque on the rotor equals the moment of inertia times angular acceleration plus the viscous friction torque:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = T_m(t)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The inertia $J$ resists changes in motion (acceleration term), and viscous friction $B$ resists motion itself (velocity term). The right-hand side is the driving torque from the electromagnetic field.

### Step 3: Combine the Two Equations

Substitute the torque equation from Step 1 into the mechanical equation from Step 2:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_c(t) - K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We now have an equation purely in terms of the input $V_c(t)$ and output $\theta(t)$, with all the physical constants of the motor.

### Step 4: Rearrange into Standard Form

Bring all the $\theta$ terms to the left-hand side:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + (B + K_b) \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_c(t)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Notice that $K_b$ (the electrical damping from back-EMF) and $B$ (the mechanical viscous damping) are now **additively combined** into a single effective damping term. This is a key conceptual point.

### Step 5: Apply the Laplace Transform

Taking the Laplace transform of both sides, assuming zero initial conditions $\theta(0) = 0$ and $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}\right\} + \mathcal{L}\left\{(B + K_b) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}\right\} = \mathcal{L}\{K_t \cdot V_c(t)\}$$

This yields:

$$J \cdot s^2 \cdot \theta(s) + (B + K_b) \cdot s \cdot \theta(s) = K_t \cdot V_c(s)$$

**Conversion Logic:** In the s-domain, derivatives become multiplications by $s$, and integrals become divisions by $s$. Initial conditions are zero, so no extra terms appear.

### Step 6: Factor Out $\theta(s)$ and Solve for the Transfer Function

Factor $\theta(s)$ on the left side:

$$\theta(s) \cdot \left[ J \cdot s^2 + (B + K_b) \cdot s \right] = K_t \cdot V_c(s)$$

Divide both sides by $V_c(s)$ and by the bracketed expression:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{J \cdot s^2 + (B + K_b) \cdot s}$$

### Step 7: Normalize to Standard Time-Constant Form

Factor out $(B + K_b)$ from the denominator:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(B + K_b) \cdot s \cdot \left[\frac{J}{B + K_b} \cdot s + 1\right]}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We are now rearranging the expression so that the denominator has the canonical form $s(\tau s + 1)$, where $\tau$ is the time constant of the system.

### Step 8: Define the Standard Motor Parameters

Define:

$$\tau = \frac{J}{B + K_b} \quad \text{(Motor Time Constant, in seconds)}$$

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{B + K_b} \quad \text{(Motor Gain Constant, in rad/V·s)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** $K_m$ represents how much angular velocity the motor produces per unit of control voltage in steady state. $\tau$ represents how quickly the motor responds to changes in input.

### Step 9: Final Standard Transfer Function

Substituting the definitions from Step 8:

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}}$$

This is the **final, canonical transfer function** of the AC Servo Motor, expressing the relationship between rotor angular position and control phase voltage. This is the answer you write in your KTU lab record and exam answer sheet.

---

## Derivation B: Derivation of the Speed Transfer Function (For Comparison)

If the output of interest is the **angular velocity** $\omega(t) = \dfrac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$ instead of position, then in the Laplace domain $\omega(s) = s \cdot \theta(s)$.

Starting from the position transfer function:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Multiplying both sides by $s$:

$$\frac{s \cdot \theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(\tau s + 1)}$$

Since $s \cdot \theta(s) = \omega(s)$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(\tau s + 1)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The speed transfer function is a **first-order lag** with steady-state gain $K_m$ and time constant $\tau$. When a step voltage $V_c$ is applied, the motor speed rises exponentially to a final value of $K_m \cdot V_c$ with time constant $\tau$.

---

## Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A two-phase AC servo motor has the following parameters:
- $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/V
- $K_b = 0.02$ N·m·s/rad
- $J = 0.001$ kg·m²
- $B = 0.005$ N·m·s/rad
- A control voltage of $V_c = 10$ V (DC equivalent) is applied.

**Find:** (a) The transfer function, (b) The time constant, (c) The steady-state speed.

### Solution

**Part (a): Transfer Function**

The effective damping is:

$$B_{eff} = B + K_b = 0.005 + 0.02 = 0.025 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

The time constant is:

$$\tau = \frac{J}{B_{eff}} = \frac{0.001}{0.025} = 0.04 \text{ seconds}$$

The motor gain constant is:

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{B_{eff}} = \frac{0.05}{0.025} = 2 \text{ rad/V·s}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{2}{s(0.04 s + 1)}$$

**Part (b): Time Constant**

$$\tau = 0.04 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}$$

**Part (c): Steady-State Speed**

For a step input of $V_c = 10$ V in the speed transfer function, by the final value theorem:

$$\omega_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_m}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{V_c}{s} = K_m \cdot V_c = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$\omega_{ss} = 20 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 191 \text{ RPM}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block Diagram of the AC Servo Motor System

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the AC Servo Motor, mapping the flow of signals from the electrical input to the mechanical output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputVc["CONTROL VOLTAGE Vc t"] --> gainKt["TORQUE GAIN Kt"]
    gainKt --> sumNode["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    speedFB["BACK EMF FEEDBACK Kb times omega"] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> torqueOut["ELECTROMAGNETIC TORQUE Tm"]
    torqueOut --> inertiaBlock["ROTATIONAL MECHANICS J s squared plus B s"]
    inertiaBlock --> positionOut["ANGULAR POSITION theta t"]
    positionOut --> derivBlock["DIFFERENTIATOR s"]
    derivBlock --> speedSig["ANGULAR VELOCITY omega t"]
    speedSig --> speedFB
    positionOut --> outputTheta["OUTPUT theta s"]
    style inputVc fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style sumNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style inertiaBlock fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputTheta fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## Signal Flow Graph (SFG) Representation

The signal flow graph is an alternative, more compact representation of the same system, using nodes and directed branches with transmittances.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Vc s"] -->|Kt| nodeB["Tm s"]
    nodeB -->|1 over Js squared plus Bs| nodeC["theta s"]
    nodeC -->|s| nodeD["omega s"]
    nodeD -->|Kb| nodeB
    style nodeA fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## Complete Closed-Loop Position Control System (Contextual Architecture)

The AC Servo Motor is rarely used in isolation. In a typical control system lab experiment, it is part of a closed-loop position servo where the motor drives a potentiometric error detector. The following Mermaid block shows this complete architecture:

```mermaid
graph LR
    refInput["REFERENCE INPUT theta ref"] --> comparator["ERROR DETECTOR COMPARATOR"]
    fbPath["FEEDBACK POTENTIOMETER"] --> comparator
    comparator --> ctrlAmp["CONTROLLER AMPLIFIER Gc"]
    ctrlAmp --> powerAmp["POWER AMPLIFIER"]
    powerAmp --> servoMotor["AC SERVO MOTOR Km over s times tau s plus 1"]
    servoMotor --> gearTrain["GEAR TRAIN"]
    gearTrain --> outputShaft["OUTPUT SHAFT theta t"]
    outputShaft --> fbPath
    style refInput fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style comparator fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style servoMotor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outputShaft fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define the transfer function of an AC servo motor. Why is it classified as a Type-1 system?**

**Model Answer:**

The transfer function of an AC servo motor is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output angular position $\theta(s)$ to the Laplace transform of the input control voltage $V_c(s)$, with all initial conditions taken as zero.

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

It is classified as a **Type-1 system** because there is exactly **one pole at the origin** (one factor of $s$ in the denominator). Physically, this "1/s" factor arises because the motor integrates the input voltage twice — once to convert voltage to torque, and once to convert torque to angular position. The presence of this integrator ensures **zero steady-state position error** for a step input, which is the defining characteristic of a Type-1 system. **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q: List any three advantages of using a two-phase AC servo motor in control system applications over a conventional DC servo motor.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **No Brushes or Commutators:** AC servo motors have no brushes, eliminating sparking, brush wear, and the need for periodic maintenance. This makes them suitable for **explosive or hazardous environments** (e.g., petrochemical plants, mining).

2. **Higher Reliability and Longer Life:** Without mechanical commutators, AC servo motors have a longer operational life and higher reliability compared to DC servo motors.

3. **Better High-Frequency Response:** The low rotor inertia (often a thin cup or drum) gives AC servo motors a **faster dynamic response** and better high-frequency performance, making them ideal for fast position control applications.

4. **Less Radio Frequency Interference (RFI):** The absence of brush commutation reduces electrical noise and RFI, which is critical in sensitive electronic control systems.

**[Any three for 3 marks — 1 mark each]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model — Choice 1]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the transfer function of a two-phase AC servo motor relating the angular position of the rotor to the control winding voltage. State the significance of the motor gain constant $K_m$ and the time constant $\tau$ in the response of the system.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Torque constant: $K_t$ (N·m/V)
- Back-EMF constant: $K_b$ (N·m·s/rad)
- Rotor inertia: $J$ (kg·m²)
- Viscous friction: $B$ (N·m·s/rad)

**Step 1: Electromagnetic Torque Equation**

The torque developed by the motor, after linearization, is:

$$T_m = K_t \cdot V_c - K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 2: Mechanical Load Equation**

By Newton's second law for rotation:

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = T_m$$

**Step 3: Combining**

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_c - K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

$$J \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + (B + K_b) \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = K_t \cdot V_c$$

**Step 4: Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions)**

$$J s^2 \theta(s) + (B + K_b) s \theta(s) = K_t V_c(s)$$

**Step 5: Solve for Transfer Function**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_t}{J s^2 + (B + K_b) s} = \frac{K_t / (B + K_b)}{s \left[ \frac{J}{B + K_b} s + 1 \right]}$$

**Step 6: Define Standard Parameters**

$$\tau = \frac{J}{B + K_b}, \quad K_m = \frac{K_t}{B + K_b}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**[7 Marks — 1 mark for torque equation, 1 for mechanical equation, 1 for combining, 1 for Laplace, 1 for solving, 1 for defining parameters, 1 for final answer]**

#### Part (b) — Significance of Parameters [7 Marks]

**Significance of Motor Gain Constant $K_m$:**

$K_m$ represents the **steady-state angular velocity** of the motor per unit of applied control voltage. Physically, it is the slope of the linearized steady-state speed-voltage characteristic. A higher $K_m$ means the motor produces more speed for a given control voltage, making the system more **responsive** but potentially less stable. In control system design, $K_m$ directly multiplies into the open-loop gain, so increasing it improves the system's disturbance rejection but reduces the phase margin and may cause oscillations. **[3.5 Marks]**

**Significance of Time Constant $\tau$:**

The time constant $\tau$ represents the **speed of response** of the motor. It is the time required for the rotor to reach **63.2%** of its final angular velocity when a step voltage is applied (in the speed transfer function). A smaller $\tau$ indicates a **faster response**, which is desirable in high-performance servo systems. However, $\tau$ depends on the rotor inertia $J$ and damping $(B + K_b)$; reducing inertia speeds up the response, while increasing damping slows it down. The time constant directly affects the **transient response** (rise time, settling time) of any closed-loop position control system. **[3.5 Marks]**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Choice 2]`

**Q: An AC servo motor has the following specifications: $K_t = 0.1$ N·m/V, $K_b = 0.05$ N·m·s/rad, $J = 0.002$ kg·m², $B = 0.01$ N·m·s/rad. Determine: (a) The transfer function $\theta(s)/V_c(s)$, and (b) The time response of the motor to a step input of 5 V applied to the control winding. Sketch the angular position output.**

#### Part (a) — Transfer Function Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute the Effective Damping**

$$B_{eff} = B + K_b = 0.01 + 0.05 = 0.06 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Time Constant**

$$\tau = \frac{J}{B_{eff}} = \frac{0.002}{0.06} = 0.0333 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Motor Gain Constant**

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{B_{eff}} = \frac{0.1}{0.06} = 1.667 \text{ rad/V·s}$$

**Step 4: Write the Transfer Function**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{1.667}{s(0.0333 s + 1)}$$

**Step 5: Express in Standard Form**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \frac{1.667}{s(0.0333 s + 1)} \text{ rad/V}$$

**[7 Marks — 1 for each computational step, 1 for final transfer function]**

#### Part (b) — Time Response to Step Input [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Apply the Step Input**

For $V_c(t) = 5 u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step), the Laplace transform is $V_c(s) = 5/s$.

The position output in the s-domain:

$$\theta(s) = \frac{1.667}{s(0.0333 s + 1)} \cdot \frac{5}{s} = \frac{8.335}{s^2 (0.0333 s + 1)}$$

**Step 2: Partial Fraction Decomposition**

Rewriting:

$$\theta(s) = \frac{8.335 / 0.0333}{s^2 (s + 30)} = \frac{250.15}{s^2 (s + 30)}$$

Partial fractions:

$$\frac{250.15}{s^2 (s + 30)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s + 30}$$

Multiplying both sides by $s^2 (s + 30)$:

$$250.15 = A \cdot s(s + 30) + B \cdot (s + 30) + C \cdot s^2$$

Setting $s = 0$: $250.15 = 30 B \Rightarrow B = 8.338$ rad.

Setting $s = -30$: $250.15 = 900 C \Rightarrow C = 0.278$ rad.

Comparing $s^2$ coefficients: $0 = A + C \Rightarrow A = -0.278$ rad.

**Step 3: Inverse Laplace Transform**

$$\theta(t) = -0.278 + 8.338 t + 0.278 e^{-30 t} \text{ for } t \geq 0$$

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

The output consists of:
- A **linear ramp term** $8.338 t$ — the rotor continues to rotate (in steady state, since there is no load torque to stop it).
- A **constant offset** $-0.278$ rad — small initial transient.
- An **exponential decay term** $0.278 e^{-30t}$ — the natural response that dies out in approximately $5\tau \approx 0.167$ seconds.

**Step 5: Sketch**

The position response is a **ramp with a transient** — the rotor accelerates briefly, then continues rotating at a constant speed of $8.338$ rad/s (≈ 80 RPM) in the steady state. The exponential transient dies out within about **0.17 seconds**.

**[7 Marks — 1 for step 1, 2 for partial fractions, 2 for inverse Laplace, 2 for interpretation and sketch]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to combine $B$ and $K_b$:** Many students treat $B$ and $K_b$ as the same symbol and write the time constant as $J/B$ or $J/K_b$ separately. The correct expression is $\tau = J/(B + K_b)$. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Missing the $1/s$ in the denominator:** Students often confuse the position transfer function $\frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ with the speed transfer function $\frac{K_m}{(\tau s + 1)}$. Remember: position needs integration, speed does not. **Penalty: -1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting zero initial conditions:** When applying the Laplace transform, the terms $s \cdot \theta(0)$ and $s^2 \cdot \dot{\theta}(0)$ must be **explicitly stated as zero** in the solution. **Penalty: -0.5 mark.**
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the 90° phase shift:** In definitions, students often forget to mention the **series capacitor** in the reference winding that creates the required 90° phase shift. This is a 1-mark deduction in Part A definitions.
>
> 5. **Wrong unit of $K_m$:** The correct unit of motor gain is **rad/V·s** (radians per volt-second), not rad/V. This is a frequently tested minor detail.
>
> 6. **No block diagram:** A 14-mark derivation question **without** a neat block diagram loses 1-2 marks. Always draw the block diagram first, then derive.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AC Servo Motor = Two-Phase Induction Motor** designed for control applications with no dead zone.
- **Two windings:** Reference (fixed, with series capacitor for 90° shift) and Control (variable amplitude).
- **Linearized Torque Equation:** $T_m = K_t V_c - K_b \omega$ — the foundation of the entire derivation.
- **Mechanical Equation:** $J \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} = T_m$ — Newton's second law for rotation.
- **Combined Equation:** $J \ddot{\theta} + (B + K_b) \dot{\theta} = K_t V_c$ — this is the key step.
- **Transfer Function (Position):** $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$
- **Transfer Function (Speed):** $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_c(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{(\tau s + 1)}$
- **Time Constant:** $\tau = \dfrac{J}{B + K_b}$ — characterizes how fast the motor responds.
- **Motor Gain:** $K_m = \dfrac{K_t}{B + K_b}$ — characterizes the sensitivity (speed per volt).
- **Type-1 System:** The "1/s" in the position transfer function makes it a Type-1 system with **zero steady-state error** for step inputs.
- **Steady-State Speed:** $\omega_{ss} = K_m \cdot V_c$ for a step input (in the speed transfer function).
- **Applications:** Robotics, CNC, aerospace, camera autofocus, HDD head positioning, antenna tracking.
- **Pole Locations:** One pole at $s = 0$ (integrator), one pole at $s = -1/\tau$ (first-order lag).
- **Physical Insight:** Rotor inertia $J$ slows down the response (large $J$ = large $\tau$); high damping $(B+K_b)$ also slows it down but improves stability.
- **Lab Tip:** To experimentally determine $K_m$ and $\tau$, apply a step voltage $V_c$ to the control winding, measure the speed response on a CRO or data acquisition system, and fit the exponential curve $K_m V_c (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ to the measured data.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (5 minutes before exam):**
> Memorize the three equations: (1) Torque equation, (2) Mechanical equation, (3) Combined equation. Once these are memorized, the transfer function follows by inspection. Draw the block diagram showing the feedback loop with $K_b$ as the feedback element. This single diagram covers 80% of the questions asked on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchro Transmitter and Receiver for Open Loop Position Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL607 - CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Synchro Transmitter and Receiver for Open Loop Position Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Synchro** is a rotary position transducer that operates on the principle of an electrical transformer with a rotating primary (rotor) and three stationary secondaries (stators), mutually coupled through a common magnetic circuit. It is essentially a small **AC servo motor** designed specifically for precise angle data transmission rather than continuous rotation.

In the context of **Open-Loop Position Control**, a **Synchro Transmitter-Receiver Pair** forms a closed electrical-mechanical coupling where:

- The **Synchro Transmitter (CX or Control Transmitter)** converts the mechanical angular position of its input shaft into a set of three proportional, time-multiplexed AC voltages across its stator terminals (S1, S2, S3).
- The **Synchro Receiver (CT or Control Transformer)** receives these voltages on its own stators, and the induced voltage across its rotor terminals becomes a function of the angular *difference* between the transmitter shaft and the receiver shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** A single device used as a transmitter is often labeled **CX** (Control Transmitter). A single device used as a receiver is often labeled **CT** (Control Transformer). When wired together, the system is generically called a **Synchro Pair** or **Synchro Link**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two **electrically connected steering wheels** in two separate cars (one in the lab, one in a remote test chamber). When the driver in the first car turns their wheel by 30 degrees, the wheel in the second car automatically turns by 30 degrees as well — without any mechanical rod or cable between them. The communication happens entirely through three thin copper wires carrying alternating current.

This is exactly how a synchro pair works. The wires (S1, S2, S3) carry information about *angle*, not *power*. The rotor of the receiver is a light indicator pointer, and as long as it is free to move, it physically follows the angle of the transmitter rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Highlight:** A synchro pair alone is an **open-loop** position indicator (like a "meter" with no feedback to the controller). It is widely used as the **error detector** inside a *closed-loop* servo system (called a **Synchro Control System**) because the difference voltage it produces is proportional to the angular error between two shafts.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Excitation (Reference) Voltage** | **115 V RMS, 50/60 Hz** | Standard KTU lab synchros |
| **Stator (Secondary) Voltage** | **49–90 V RMS** at maximum coupling | Function of shaft angle |
| **Rotor (Primary) Current** | **~0.3 A** nominal | Depends on size of synchro |
| **Accuracy Class** | **±0.1° to ±1°** | Military-grade up to industrial |
| **Stator Configuration** | **3-phase Y (Star) connected** | Windings spaced 120° mechanically |
| **Operating Temperature** | **−55°C to +125°C** | Standard military (MIL-S-20708) |
| **Stator Winding Spacing** | **120° electrical** | Three phases A, B, C |

> [!NOTE]
> The **50 Hz** excitation is dominant in **KTU Kerala labs**, as the Indian power supply standard is **50 Hz**, in contrast to the US-inherited **60 Hz** military specs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A synchro is structurally identical to a **3-phase wound-rotor induction motor**, but the rotor is fed with single-phase AC via slip rings and brushes.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Rotor Excitation:** The rotor winding (single-phase) is energized with an AC reference voltage of the form:
$$V_{r} = V_{rm} \sin(\omega t)$$
This produces a pulsating magnetic flux along the rotor axis.

2. **Flux Linkage to Stators:** This alternating flux links with the **three stator windings (S1–S2, S2–S3, S3–S1)** that are displaced 120° apart in space.

3. **Induced EMF per Winding:** By Faraday's law, the flux through each stator coil depends on the cosine of the angle between the rotor axis and the stator axis. If the rotor is at shaft angle **θ**, the induced stator voltages are:
$$V_{S1} = K \cdot V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$
$$V_{S2} = K \cdot V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$$
$$V_{S3} = K \cdot V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$$

   where **K** is the transformation ratio of the synchro (turns ratio).

4. **Why "Cosine" and not "Sine"?** By convention, when the rotor axis is **aligned with the S2 winding axis**, the maximum coupling occurs, giving maximum voltage on S2. So the coupling coefficient uses the cosine of the angular difference.

5. **Transmitter-to-Receiver Transfer:** When the S1, S2, S3 terminals of the transmitter are connected to the corresponding terminals of the receiver, currents flow that produce their **own** magnetic field in the receiver. The receiver rotor experiences a torque proportional to the angular *error*:
$$\tau_{net} = T_{max} \sin(\theta_{t} - \theta_{r})$$

6. **Open-Loop Tracking:** If the receiver rotor is free, this torque physically rotates the receiver rotor until $\theta_r = \theta_t$, at which point the error torque becomes zero and equilibrium is reached. This is the principle of **open-loop position control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The receiver shaft in a synchro pair ALWAYS tries to follow the transmitter shaft. The induced voltage at the receiver rotor terminals is a direct measure of the **angular error** $\theta_e = \theta_t - \theta_r$, making the synchro a natural **error-sensing transducer** for servo systems.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_r = V_{rm} \sin(\omega t)$ | Rotor excitation (reference) | Volts |
| 2 | $V_{S1} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$ | Stator S1 voltage | Volts (RMS / peak) |
| 3 | $V_{S2} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$ | Stator S2 voltage | Volts |
| 4 | $V_{S3} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$ | Stator S3 voltage | Volts |
| 5 | $V_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{phase}$ | Line-to-line stator voltage | Volts |
| 6 | $e(\theta_e) = K \sin(\theta_t - \theta_r) \sin(\omega t)$ | Error voltage at CT rotor | Volts (line-to-line) |
| 7 | $\theta_e = \theta_t - \theta_r$ | Angular error | Degrees or radians |
| 8 | $\tau_{net} = T_{max} \sin(\theta_t - \theta_r)$ | Restoring torque on receiver | N·m |
| 9 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency of excitation | rad/s |
| 10 | $K = \dfrac{N_s}{N_r}$ | Turns ratio (stator / rotor) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $V_{max} = K \cdot V_{rm}$ | Peak stator coupling voltage | Volts |
| 12 | $e_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{line}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS error voltage | Volts |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The **error voltage** is a sinusoidal function of the *angular error*. For small errors, it is approximately linear: $e \approx K \sin(\theta_e) \approx K \theta_e$. This is what makes the synchro usable as a linear error sensor in a closed-loop servo. Students often confuse the **error voltage** with the **stator voltage** — they are different physical quantities measured at different terminals.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of Synchro Pair |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Radar Antenna Tracking** | Transmits antenna angle from platform to control room |
| **Robotic Joint Sensing** | Acts as analog absolute encoder for low-cost joints |
| **Aircraft Control Surfaces** | Reports flap, rudder, and aileron position to cockpit |
| **Gun Fire-Control Systems** | Computes firing solution based on target bearing |
| **Machine Tool Position Indication** | Digital readout of large rotary tables |
| **Hydraulic Valve Actuator Feedback** | Provides shaft-position feedback to electronic controller |
| **Inertial Navigation Systems (older)** | Heading indicator coupling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stator Voltage Expressions for a Synchro Transmitter

### Step 1: Define the Rotor Excitation

Let the single-phase rotor winding be energized by a reference AC source. The applied voltage is sinusoidal in time:

$$V_{r}(t) = V_{rm} \sin(\omega t)$$

This produces a sinusoidally alternating flux **Φ** that is **fixed in space** along the rotor axis but **sinusoidal in time**:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_{m} \sin(\omega t)$$

The direction of **Φ** is the direction of the rotor axis (i.e., the direction the rotor points to in space at angle θ).

### Step 2: Define the Stator Winding Geometry

The three stator windings (S1, S2, S3) are wound on three identical poles (or in slots) that are displaced **120° apart** in space around the air gap. Let us define the angular position of the rotor with respect to a fixed reference axis as **θ**.

The S1 winding axis makes an angle **θ** with respect to the reference axis.

The S2 winding axis makes an angle **(θ − 120°)** with respect to the reference axis.

The S3 winding axis makes an angle **(θ + 120°)** with respect to the reference axis.

### Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law to Each Stator

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the EMF induced in a coil is equal to the negative rate of change of the magnetic flux linking that coil:

$$e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

The flux linking stator winding **Si** is the projection of the rotor flux **Φ** onto the axis of that winding:

$$\Phi_{Si} = \Phi(t) \cos(\theta_i)$$

where $\theta_i$ is the angular offset between the rotor axis and the Si winding axis.

So for S1 (offset = 0°):

$$\Phi_{S1} = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$

For S2 (offset = −120°):

$$\Phi_{S2} = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$$

For S3 (offset = +120°):

$$\Phi_{S3} = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$$

### Step 4: Compute the Induced EMFs

Differentiating with respect to time (assuming $N_s$ turns per stator coil and a constant $K$):

$$V_{S1} = N_s \frac{d\Phi_{S1}}{dt} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta)$$

$$V_{S2} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta - 120°)$$

$$V_{S3} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cos(\theta + 120°)$$

This is the **fundamental synchro voltage equation**, which is **mandatory** in any KTU derivation question.

## 3.2 Derivation: Error Voltage at the Receiver Rotor

### Step 1: Stator Currents Driven by Transmitter

When the transmitter and receiver are interconnected through their S1, S2, S3 lines, the stator voltages from the transmitter circulate currents through the receiver's stator windings. These currents have the same time waveform but a magnitude and phase that depend on the receiver's shaft angle $\theta_r$.

The **net rotor voltage** (error voltage) at the receiver rotor terminals is the projection of the resultant stator MMF onto the rotor axis. Through a careful phasor analysis:

$$V_{rotor}^{recv} = K V_{rm} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta_t - \theta_r)$$

where $\theta_t$ is the transmitter shaft angle and $\theta_r$ is the receiver shaft angle.

### Step 2: Define the Angular Error

Let us define the angular error:

$$\theta_e = \theta_t - \theta_r$$

Substituting:

$$V_{error} = K V_{rm} \sin(\theta_e) \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: RMS Value of Error Voltage

Taking the RMS (or the amplitude of the AC component) by integrating over one period:

$$V_{error,rms} = \frac{K V_{rm}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \vert \sin(\theta_e) \vert$$

For small errors (a few degrees), $\sin(\theta_e) \approx \theta_e$ in radians, giving a **linear** error voltage proportional to angular displacement.

### Step 4: Small-Signal Sensitivity

The slope of the error voltage at the null point ($\theta_e = 0$) gives the **static sensitivity** of the synchro pair:

$$K_s = \left. \frac{dV_{error}}{d\theta_e} \right\rvert_{\theta_e = 0} = K V_{rm} \text{ V/rad}$$

For practical synchros, this is on the order of **1–5 V per degree** of angular error.

## 3.3 Derivation: Transfer Function Model for the Synchro Error Detector

In a closed-loop servo, the synchro pair is modeled as a **proportional gain** in the small-signal regime. From the lab data sheet approach:

Let the input to the transmitter be a step displacement $\theta_t(t)$, and let the receiver follow with some lag due to its mechanical inertia $J_r$ and viscous friction $B_r$.

The equation of motion of the receiver rotor is:

$$J_r \ddot{\theta}_r + B_r \dot{\theta}_r = T_{max} \sin(\theta_t - \theta_r)$$

Linearizing for small $\theta_e$:

$$J_r \ddot{\theta}_r + B_r \dot{\theta}_r = T_{max} \cdot \theta_e$$

Since $\theta_e = \theta_t - \theta_r$:

$$J_r \ddot{\theta}_e + B_r \dot{\theta}_e + T_{max} \cdot \theta_e = J_r \ddot{\theta}_t + B_r \dot{\theta}_t$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$(J_r s^2 + B_r s + T_{max}) \theta_e(s) = (J_r s + B_r) s \cdot \theta_t(s)$$

Therefore, the **transfer function** from transmitter input to angular error is:

$$\frac{\theta_e(s)}{\theta_t(s)} = \frac{s(J_r s + B_r)}{J_r s^2 + B_r s + T_{max}}$$

The natural frequency and damping ratio of the receiver are:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{T_{max}}{J_r}} \quad \text{and} \quad \zeta = \frac{B_r}{2\sqrt{J_r T_{max}}}$$

This is a **second-order system** that is the foundation for synchro-based servo design — a **classic KTU viva question**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Synchro Operating Modes

| Mode | Rotor Connection | Stator Connection | Output | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Torque Transmission** | Energized (TX), Free (RX) | Wired TX ↔ RX | Mechanical alignment | Remote indicator, light dial |
| **Control Transformer (CT)** | Energized (TX), Output taken (RX) | Wired TX ↔ RX | Error voltage | Error detector in servo loop |
| **Differential Transmitter** | Energized, 3-phase out | Two TX inputs added | Sum/difference of angles | Coordinate transform / adder |
| **Differential Receiver** | Energized | Takes two TX signals | Angle difference on dial | Subtraction indicator |
| **Resolver** | Two perpendicular rotor coils | Two perpendicular stators | $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ | Cartesian ↔ polar conversion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Synchro Transmitter-Receiver Pair — Physical Layout

```
    TRANSMITTER (CX)                  RECEIVER (CT)
   ===================               ===================
   R1 o────/ ──── AC                  o──── AC
         (rotor)                            (rotor)
        θ_t  |                              |
   ┌────────┼────────┐            ┌────────┼────────┐
   │   S1   ●S2      │            │   S1   ●S2      │
   │         ●S3     │            │         ●S3     │
   └─────────────────┘            └─────────────────┘
   Stator Y-wound,                Stator Y-wound,
   120° apart                      120° apart
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input Shaft theta_t] --> B[Transmitter Rotor Winding]
    B --> C[Excited by AC Reference V_rm sin omega t]
    C --> D[Three Stator Windings S1 S2 S3 with 120 deg spacing]
    D --> E[Three Stator Voltages V_S1 V_S2 V_S3 proportional to cos theta]
    E --> F[Interconnecting Three-Wire Bus]
    F --> G[Receiver Stator Windings S1 S2 S3]
    G --> H[Resultant MMF in Receiver Air Gap]
    H --> I[Error Voltage on Receiver Rotor proportional to sin theta_e]
    I --> J{Open Loop Indicator Mode}
    J -->|Yes| K[Receiver Shaft Rotates to Match theta_t]
    J -->|No| L[Error Voltage Read Out to Amplifier]
    L --> M[Servo Motor Driver]
    M --> N[Closes the Loop Back to theta_t]
```

## 4.3 Detailed Signal Flow in Open-Loop Position Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[/Mechanical Angle theta_t/] --> excite[Apply 115 V 50 Hz AC to Rotor]
    excite --> coupling[Magnetic Flux phi = phi_m sin omega t]
    coupling --> stator1[Stator S1 voltage V_S1 = K V sin omega t cos theta]
    coupling --> stator2[Stator S2 voltage V_S2 = K V sin omega t cos theta minus 120]
    coupling --> stator3[Stator S3 voltage V_S3 = K V sin omega t cos theta plus 120]
    stator1 --> bus[Common 3-wire Bus]
    stator2 --> bus
    stator3 --> bus
    bus --> recv[Receiver Stator Windings]
    recv --> mmf[Resultant Stator MMF in Receiver]
    mmf --> torque[Net Torque T max sin theta_e on Receiver Rotor]
    torque --> shaft[Receiver Shaft Aligns with Transmitter]
    shaft --> out[/Output Dial Reads theta_t/]
```

## 4.4 Experimental Setup Diagram (KTU Lab)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[230 V 50 Hz Variac and Step Down Transformer to 115 V 50 Hz] --> EXC[Synchro Transmitter Rotor R1 R2]
    EXC --> TX[Transmitter Stator S1 S2 S3]
    TX -->|Three-Wire Link| RX[Receiver Stator S1 S2 S3]
    RX --> RRO[Receiver Rotor Output R1 R2]
    RRO --> DMM[Digital Multimeter for Stator Voltage Measurement]
    RRO --> OSC[Oscilloscope for Error Voltage Waveform]
    TXSHAFT[Transmitter Hand Crank with Protractor Dial] --> EXC
    RXSHAFT[Receiver Pointer Dial with Protractor] --> RX
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Note:** In a typical KTU lab, the **transmitter** is mounted on a hand-cranked rotary fixture calibrated in degrees, and the **receiver** is a panel-mounted unit with a pointer that physically moves. The student is expected to record the stator voltage at 0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°, and so on, and verify the **cosine law** from Section 3.1.
